skill.name: cqs-patterns
skill.description: Command Query Separation (CQS) and CQRS patterns for .NET. Use when designing methods, handlers, and application architecture. Ensures predictable, testable code.
skill.summary: Apply CQS and CQRS design patterns to .NET methods, handlers, and application architecture.
skill.author: DoubleslashSE

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns in SKILL.md. Manual review found these are false positives from Markdown code fences, CQS/CQRS terminology, SQL ordering text, and C# example method names. No malicious instructions, data exfiltration, network access, or prompt injection attempts were found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Detected as Commands
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are Markdown code fences for diagrams and C# examples. They do not execute commands and do not instruct the agent to run shell code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious characters are fenced Markdown delimiters around documentation examples. There is no executable Ruby, shell command, or instruction to invoke a command interpreter.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Keyword Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts are keyword matches in normal documentation, including CQS terminology and SQL ordering text. No cryptographic API, hashing function, cipher, or credential handling appears in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines contain architecture description, SQL sort direction, and a benefits table. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or similar cryptographic implementation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Terms in C# Examples
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alerts are ordinary C# method names, routing examples, and architecture prose. They describe application reads and writes rather than host discovery or environment enumeration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is a .NET CQS/CQRS tutorial using words such as Get, Update, Delete, and Count. I found no commands or instructions for host reconnaissance.

content.user_title: Apply CQS and CQRS Patterns in .NET
content.value_statement: Mixed read and write logic can make .NET code harder to test and reason about. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through CQS and CQRS patterns for clearer methods, handlers, and controllers.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to separate commands that change state from queries that return data.
Provides .NET method-level examples for CQS violations and corrected designs.
Shows CQRS command, query, handler, dispatcher, and controller patterns.
Documents acceptable exceptions such as atomic operations, fluent APIs, and factory methods.
Helps decide when CQRS fits complex domains and when simple CRUD is enough.
limitations: Focuses on .NET examples and may need adaptation for other languages.
Does not provide a complete production framework or package.
Does not cover event sourcing implementation in depth.
Requires project-specific judgment before applying CQRS to simple systems.

use_cases.title: Refactor Service Methods
use_cases.description: Identify methods that both mutate state and return data, then split them into clearer commands and queries.

use_cases.title: Design CQRS Handlers
use_cases.description: Create command and query handler boundaries for application features with separate write and read models.

use_cases.title: Review Architecture Choices
use_cases.description: Evaluate whether CQRS adds value for a domain or adds unnecessary complexity to a simple CRUD application.

prompt_templates.title: Check a Method for CQS
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the cqs-patterns skill to review this .NET method. Tell me whether it is a command, a query, or a CQS violation. Suggest a clearer design.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor a Service Class
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the cqs-patterns skill to review this service class. Separate command behavior from query behavior and explain the method naming changes.

prompt_templates.title: Design Feature Handlers
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the cqs-patterns skill to design command records, query records, handlers, and controller actions for this .NET feature.

prompt_templates.title: Assess CQRS Fit
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the cqs-patterns skill to assess whether this system should use CQRS. Compare domain complexity, read and write patterns, team size, and operational cost.

output_examples.input: Review a user creation method that saves a user and returns the full user object.
output_examples.output: The method is a CQS violation because it changes state and returns domain data. A clearer design is to return only the new identifier from the command and provide a separate query to fetch the user.
The output would define a cancellation command, a command handler that loads and updates the order, and a separate query path for reading order details.

output_examples.input: Decide whether a small CRUD admin panel needs CQRS.
output_examples.output: The recommendation would usually avoid CQRS because simple CRUD workflows do not benefit enough from separate read and write models.

best_practices: Use imperative names for commands and descriptive names for queries.
Keep queries free of side effects so they remain safe to repeat.
Apply CQRS when domain complexity or read and write scaling justify the added structure.
anti_patterns: Do not hide state changes inside property getters or query methods.
Do not apply CQRS to simple CRUD workflows without a clear reason.
Do not let command handlers return read models when a separate query would be clearer.

faq.question: What is CQS?
faq.answer: CQS means a method should either change state or return data, but not do both.

faq.question: What is CQRS?
faq.answer: CQRS applies CQS at the architecture level by separating write commands from read queries.

faq.question: Does this skill only support .NET?
faq.answer: The examples are written for .NET and C#, but the design principles can be adapted elsewhere.

faq.question: When should I avoid CQRS?
faq.answer: Avoid CQRS for simple CRUD applications, small teams, or systems where the added structure has no clear payoff.

faq.question: Can commands return anything?
faq.answer: The skill treats returning an identifier from a command as acceptable, while full read data should come from a query.

faq.question: Does the skill include runnable tooling?
faq.answer: No. It is a guidance skill with examples for designing and reviewing code.
